Exploratory Data Analysis¶

In [4]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
In [5]:
sns.set_theme(style = 'whitegrid')
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (9,5)
plt.rcParams['axes.titlesize'] = 13
plt.rcParams['axes.titleweight'] = 'bold'

DATA_PATH = 'Dataset.xlsx'

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.width', 150)

1. Load Data & Overview¶

In [6]:
df = pd.read_excel(DATA_PATH)
df

2. Data Quality Checking¶

  • Check whether the TotalPrice column calculated by Quantity x UnitPrice
In [17]:
total_price = (df['Quantity'] * df['UnitPrice']).round(2)
# total_price
matching = (total_price != df['TotalPrice'].round(2))
if matching.sum() == 0:
    print('Total Price Calculated Correclty')
else:
    print('Invalid!')

Total Price Calculated Correclty
  • Check whether Quantity exceed ItemsInCart
In [18]:
qty_cart = (df['Quantity'] > df['ItemsInCart']).sum()
print('Number of orders where quantity exceed items in card is', qty_cart)

Number of orders where quantity exceed items in card is 0
  • Check whether there exist non-positive values in numeric columns
In [19]:
neg_or_zero = ((df["Quantity"] <= 0) | (df["UnitPrice"] <= 0) | (df["TotalPrice"] <= 0)).sum()
print(f"Rows with non-positive Quantity/UnitPrice/TotalPrice: {neg_or_zero}")

Rows with non-positive Quantity/UnitPrice/TotalPrice: 0
  • Date range
In [20]:
print(f"\nDate range: {df['Date'].min().date()} to {df['Date'].max().date()}")

Date range: 2023-01-01 to 2025-06-30
  • Percentage of missing values in CouponCode
In [21]:
print(f"Percentage of orders not used coupon code: {df['CouponCode'].isnull().mean()*100:.1f}%")

Percentage of orders not used coupon code: 25.8%

3. Univariate Analysis¶

3.1. Univariate Analysis for Numerical Columns¶

In [22]:
numeric_columns = ['Quantity', 'UnitPrice', 'ItemsInCart', 'TotalPrice']
df[numeric_columns].describe().round(2)
Out[22]:
Quantity UnitPrice ItemsInCart TotalPrice
count 1200.00 1200.00 1200.00 1200.00
mean 2.95 356.41 5.48 1053.97
std 1.41 197.18 2.28 819.86
min 1.00 11.39 1.00 11.39
25% 2.00 186.06 4.00 410.52
50% 3.00 364.21 5.00 823.62
75% 4.00 521.57 7.00 1578.48
max 5.00 699.93 10.00 3456.40
In [23]:
print("Skewness:")
df[numeric_columns].skew()

Skewness:
Out[23]:
Quantity       0.027922
UnitPrice     -0.026512
ItemsInCart    0.000877
TotalPrice     0.891359
dtype: float64
  • TotalPrice positively skewed.
In [35]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8)) # gives 4 grids

for sub_plot, col in zip(axes.flat, numeric_columns): 
    sns.histplot(df[col], 
                 kde=True, 
                 ax=sub_plot, 
                 color="#4C72B0")
    sub_plot.set_title(f"Distribution of {col}")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Quantity is evenly distributed across values 1-5.
  • UnitPrice shows a wide range of product prices (20 - 700) with no dominant price level.
  • ItemsInCart is centered around 5-6 items, indicating a typical cart size of approximately 5-6 products.
  • TotalPrice is positively skewed, suggesting most orders have low-to-moderate values while a small number of orders contributes very high sales amounts.
In [53]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize = (17, 5))

for sub_plot, col in zip(axes, numeric_columns):
    sns.boxplot(y = df[col],
               ax = sub_plot,
               color = "#55A868")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Quantity, UnitPrice, and ItemsInCart do not contain significant outliers and exhibit relatively balanced distributions.
  • TotalPrice shows positively skewed distribution with several high-value outliers.
In [200]:
print("Outlier counts (1.5x IQR rule):")
print()
for col in numeric_columns:
    q1, q3 = df[col].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lower, upper = q1 - 1.5 * iqr, q3 + 1.5 * iqr
    n_out = ((df[col] < lower) | (df[col] > upper)).sum()
    print(f"  {col}: {n_out} outliers (bounds: {lower:.2f} to {upper:.2f})")

Outlier counts (1.5x IQR rule):

  Quantity: 0 outliers (bounds: -1.00 to 7.00)
  UnitPrice: 0 outliers (bounds: -317.20 to 1024.83)
  ItemsInCart: 0 outliers (bounds: -0.50 to 11.50)
  TotalPrice: 8 outliers (bounds: -1341.41 to 3330.41)

In [88]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 2, figsize=(12, 14))

for sub_plot, col in zip(axes.flat, categorical_cols):

    order = df[col].value_counts().index

    sns.countplot(
        y=df[col],
        order=order,
        hue=df[col],
        ax=sub_plot,
        palette="viridis",
        legend=False
    )
    sub_plot.set_title(f"Count of Orders by {col}")
    sub_plot.set_xlabel("Count")

axes.flat[-1].axis("off") # hide the unused plot
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4. Time-Based Analysis¶

In [108]:
df["YearMonth"] = df["Date"].dt.to_period("M")

monthly = df.groupby("YearMonth").agg(
    orders=("OrderID", "count"),
    revenue=("TotalPrice", "sum"),
    avg_order_value=("TotalPrice", "mean"), # Average Order Value = Total Revenue / Number of Orders
).reset_index()

monthly["YearMonth"] = monthly["YearMonth"].astype(str)
# monthly.dtypes
monthly
Out[108]:
YearMonth orders revenue avg_order_value

In [142]:
metrics = ['orders', 'revenue', 'avg_order_value']
colors = ['#4C72B0', '#C44E52', '#55A868']

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(12, 15))

for ax, metric, color in zip(axes, metrics, colors):

    sns.lineplot(
        data=monthly,
        x='YearMonth',
        y=metric,
        marker='o',
        ax=ax,
        color = color
    )

    ax.set_title(f'Monthly {metric.replace("_", " ").title()}')
    ax.set_xlabel('Year-Month')
    ax.set_ylabel(metric.title())

    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=90)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [141]:
df['WeekDay'] = df['Date'].dt.day_name()
week_order = ["Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]
weekly_count = df['WeekDay'].value_counts().reindex(week_order)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

sns.barplot(
    x = weekly_count.index,
    y = weekly_count.values,
    hue = weekly_count.index,
    ax = ax,
    palette = 'viridis'
)

ax.set_title("Orders by Day of Week")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5. Relationships Between Variables¶

In [148]:
df.groupby("Product")["TotalPrice"]. \
    agg(["mean", "median", "sum", "count"]).round(2) \
    .sort_values("sum", ascending=False)
Out[148]:
mean median sum count
Product
Chair 1098.99 928.58 195620.11 178
Printer 1080.73 857.40 195612.61 181
Laptop 1110.56 915.64 192126.56 173
Tablet 1042.28 786.66 186568.95 179
Monitor 1077.62 853.40 175651.41 163
Desk 985.06 784.39 167459.93 170
Phone 972.58 691.71 151722.39 156

mean = average order value

  • Laptop has the highest average order value.
  • Printer is the most frequently ordered product.
In [162]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

sns.boxplot(data=df, 
            x="Product", 
            y="TotalPrice", 
            hue="Product", 
            ax=ax, 
            palette="Set3", 
            legend=False)

ax.set_title("Average Order Value Distribution by Product")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Product-level boxplots show that Chair and Laptop orders have the highest typical spending levels, while Phone orders have the lowest.
  • All product categories exhibit right-skewed distributions with several high-value outliers, indicating occasional large purchases.
In [153]:
df.groupby("ReferralSource")["TotalPrice"]. \
    agg(["mean", "median", "count"]). \
    sort_values("mean", ascending=False)

6. Correlation Between Numeric Variables¶

In [175]:
corr = df[numeric_columns].corr()
corr.round(2)
Out[175]:
Quantity UnitPrice ItemsInCart TotalPrice
Quantity 1.00 0.01 0.65 0.62
UnitPrice 0.01 1.00 0.00 0.72
ItemsInCart 0.65 0.00 1.00 0.39
TotalPrice 0.62 0.72 0.39 1.00
In [177]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))

sns.heatmap(corr, 
            annot=True, 
            cmap="coolwarm", 
            center=0, 
            ax=ax, 
            fmt=".2f")

ax.set_title("Correlation Heatmap")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7. Customer-Level Summary¶

In [179]:
cust = df.groupby("CustomerID").agg(
    n_orders=("OrderID", "count"),
    total_spend=("TotalPrice", "sum"),
    avg_order_value=("TotalPrice", "mean"),
).sort_values("total_spend", ascending=False)

print(f"Unique customers: {df['CustomerID'].nunique()}")
print(f"Returning Customers: {(cust['n_orders'] > 1).sum()}")
cust.head(10)

Unique customers: 1189
Returning Customers: 11

In [180]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

sns.histplot(cust["total_spend"], 
             bins=30, 
             color="#4C72B0", 
             kde=True, 
             ax=ax)

ax.set_title("Distribution of Total Spend per Customer")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

8. Executive Summary¶

1. Problem Statement¶

Why the order cancellation is high?

2. Methodology¶

Checked payment method, product, channel, and coupon use against cancellations, and ran proper statistical tests.

3. Key Findings¶

Cancellation rate is 20.8%, but no single factor explains it, payment method, product, channel, and coupon usage all showed no statistically significant link.

4. Recommendations¶

Check for customer-level repeat cancellations
